module: Sepsis from Tropical Diseases Part III: Critical Illness and Sepsis from Tuberculosis Disease

sections.Introduction:
When exposed to droplets containing Mycobacterium tuberculosis, 20–25% of individuals become infected, among whom 5–10% develop tuberculosis disease within a median time of 2 years. Risk factors for developing TB disease include: HIV infection, recent treatment for M. tuberculosis (within past 2 years), silicosis, chronic respiratory disease, smoking, diabetes mellitus, severe chronic renal insufficiency, solid organ transplantation, immunosuppressive therapy, malnutrition (BMI <18), pregnancy and post-partum period, illicit drug use, alcoholism, age <5 or >65 years, and vitamin D deficiency.

In 2019, 10 million new TB infections occurred worldwide; TB claimed 1.4 million lives. Acute respiratory failure, septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction are the most common ICU admission reasons. Independent risk factors for ICU death: miliary TB, mechanical ventilation, shock, nosocomial pneumonia, multi-organ failure, APACHE II >20, and duration of symptoms >4 weeks.
sections.Clinical Symptoms:
Systemic symptoms: fatigue, remittent fever, weight loss, night sweats, cervical lymphadenitis. Pulmonary TB: productive cough, dyspnoea, haemoptysis. Life-threatening haemoptysis can result from erosion of a bronchial artery or rupture of an arterial aneurysm.

Tuberculous pericarditis: dyspnoea, chest pain relieved upright; enlarged effusion causes decreased heart sounds, leg oedema, congested neck veins, pulsus paradoxus.

CNS tuberculosis: TB meningitis most frequent. Symptoms subacute and non-specific (fever, headache, malaise, vomiting). Cranial nerve palsies (especially abducens) in one third of patients.

British Medical Research Council grades of TBM:
- Grade I: GCS 15, no focal neurological deficit
- Grade II: GCS 14–10 ± focal deficit, OR GCS 15 with focal deficit
- Grade III: GCS <10 ± focal deficit

Abdominal tuberculosis complicates one third of pulmonary TB cases.

Miliary tuberculosis: haematogenous seeding. Most commonly affected organs: lungs, liver, spleen, meninges, kidneys. Characteristic skin lesions and choroidal tubercles on fundoscopy. Many patients present with ARDS, shock, DIC and multi-organ dysfunction.
sections.Diagnostic Work-up:
WHO strongly recommends Xpert® MTB/RIF or Xpert® Ultra as initial diagnostic method. Results from unprocessed samples available within <2 hours. Sensitivity >98% in smear- and culture-positive pulmonary TB. Specificity 99–100%. Simultaneously detects genomic sequences for rifampicin resistance (not isoniazid). Sensitivity falls below 50% when pleural or ascitic fluid is analysed.

Lateral flow urine lipoarabinomannan (LAM) assay: WHO strongly recommends in HIV-positive adults/children with CD4 <100/mm³, OR those who are seriously ill and hospitalised regardless of clinical symptoms.

CSF in TBM: increased lymphocytic cell count, increased protein (precipitates to 'spider's web clot' on standing), decreased glucose. PCT is usually NOT elevated in TB — low PCT may help diagnose bacterial co-infection.

Adenosine deaminase in pleural fluid: cut-off >60 IU/L supports tuberculous pleurisy. Pleural/peritoneal biopsies commonly required to confirm diagnosis.
sections.Anti-tuberculosis Therapy:
Standard regimen for drug-sensitive TB: rifampicin + isoniazid + ethambutol + pyrazinamide for 2 months (intensive phase), then rifampicin + isoniazid for 4 months. TBM treatment: 12 months.

First-line drug key properties:
- Rifampicin: 10 mg/kg/d (max 600 mg/d). NO dose adjustment in renal failure. CSF penetration 10–20%. Potent CYP INDUCER. Main risk: DILI.
- Isoniazid: 5 mg/kg/d. NO dose adjustment in renal failure. CSF penetration 100%. CYP INHIBITOR. Risks: DILI, peripheral neuropathy (prevent with pyridoxine 10–25 mg/d).
- Ethambutol: 15 mg/kg/d. DOSE ADJUSTMENT required in renal failure. CSF penetration 25–50%. Main risk: retrobulbar neuritis.
- Pyrazinamide: 25 mg/kg/d. DOSE ADJUSTMENT required in renal failure. CSF penetration 100%. Main risk: DILI, rhabdomyolysis.

DILI: most dangerous adverse effect. Incidence 3–13% (age-dependent). Triggered by rifampicin, isoniazid and/or pyrazinamide; worsened by concurrent hepatotoxic drugs (paracetamol, valproic acid). First sign: liver enzymes >3× normal.

MDR-TB (resistant to ≥isoniazid + rifampicin): do NOT add individual drugs to a failing regimen — replace entire regimen with 4–5 susceptible second-line drugs. Duration ≥18 months.

Pharmacokinetics in critical illness: administer IV until GI function restored. Rifampicin INDUCES CYP (reduces azoles, moxifloxacin, steroids, anticonvulsants, etc.). Isoniazid INHIBITS CYP. Combined: net effect is inductive (rifampicin > isoniazid).
sections.Adjunctive Therapies:
Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day IV/PO (or prednisolone 60 mg/day) reduces mortality and long-term sequelae in HIV-negative patients with TBM and pericarditis. Evidence in HIV-positive patients and pulmonary TB without CNS/pericardial involvement remains controversial.

Rifampicin increases cortisol metabolism and may precipitate adrenal insufficiency.

Prophylactic aspirin 150 mg/day reduces both stroke risk and mortality in patients with TBM.
sections.Intensive Care Pearls:
NIV failure rate is HIGH in TB-associated ARDS. Invasive lung-protective ventilation principles do not differ from other ARDS. VAP occurs in up to two-thirds of patients.

Pulmonary haemorrhage — FIRST priority: airway control and adequate gas exchange (intubation + invasive MV). Adjuncts: antitussives (opiates), tranexamic acid. Bronchoscopy (topical diluted epinephrine). CT angiography + bronchial artery embolisation highly effective. Emergency surgery reserved for failures.

TBM ICU management: intubation for airway protection. Complications: hydrocephalus (80% communicating), seizures (frequent in children ~50%, rare in adults ~5%), SIADH, stroke in up to two-thirds (mostly basal ganglia). Conservative management of communicating hydrocephalus with acetazolamide 100 mg/kg/day; repeated lumbar drainage or external ventricular drain required in one third. Valproic acid — use with caution (DILI risk).
sections.Management of HIV Co-infected Patients:
HIV is the leading cause of death among HIV-positive persons via TB (26% of AIDS-related deaths). At CD4 <75/mm³: pulmonary symptoms absent; mycobacteraemia with miliary TB common.

ART timing:
- Pulmonary TB: initiate ART ideally within 2 weeks of starting and tolerating anti-TB therapy
- Exception — TB meningitis: ART should be initiated ONLY after 8 weeks of anti-TB drugs
- Patients already on ART at TB diagnosis: continue without interruption

All TB/HIV co-infected patients should receive trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis (reduces PCP, toxoplasmosis, malaria, bacterial infections + mortality).

IRIS: typically manifests ~2 weeks after ART initiation. Mild forms in one third of patients. Severe: expanding tuberculomas, worsening ARDS. Management: steroids (prednisolone 1.5 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks, then 0.75 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks) for severe cases. Rarely interrupt ART unless life-threatening.
sections.Specific Considerations in Children:
Key differences between children and adults with TB:
- HIV co-infection: rare in children, frequent in adults
- Extrapulmonary TB: frequent in children (>30%) independent of HIV status; rare in adults (except HIV+)
- TBM — seizures: frequent in children (~50%) vs rare in adults (~5%)
- TBM — agitation/delirium: infrequent in children vs frequent in adults
- Bacillary load: low/paucibacillary in children vs low-to-high in adults
- Anti-TB treatment: 3 or 4 first-line drugs (children, drug-sensitive) vs 4 drugs (adults)
- Pharmacokinetics: children metabolise anti-TB drugs faster — higher mg/kg doses required
- Side effects: infrequent in children, frequent in adults
sections.Infection Control and Preventing Spread:
M. tuberculosis spreads by airborne droplets that remain airborne for several hours. Early diagnosis and rapid implementation of airborne isolation is the most important prevention measure.

Isolation: single room with negative pressure, air exchanged ≥6 times per hour.

Personal protective equipment:
- N95 (FFP2): required for routine room entry
- N98 (FFP3): required for aerosol-generating procedures (intubation, extubation, handling respiratory secretions)

Mechanically ventilated patients: closed suction systems and expiratory limb filters.

De-isolation: pragmatically after 2-week anti-TB course in drug-sensitive TB. Patients with drug-resistant TB, diabetes or immunosuppression may remain smear-positive longer — prolonged isolation required.

BCG vaccine: decreases risk of TBM and disseminated TB; does NOT prevent primary infection or reactivation of latent pulmonary TB.

questions.question: A 58-year-old HIV-negative man with confirmed pulmonary tuberculosis develops progressive dyspnoea and meets Berlin criteria for severe ARDS (P/F ratio 85 mmHg). Non-invasive ventilation is initiated but fails despite optimal settings within 4 hours. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Urgent referral for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO)
B: Repeat NIV trial with a different mask interface and increased PEEP
C: Invasive mechanical ventilation with a lung-protective ventilation strategy
D: Awake prone positioning without intubation
E: High-flow nasal oxygen at 60 L/min as a bridge to further assessment
questions.explanation: NIV failure rates are high in tuberculosis-associated ARDS; once NIV fails, invasive mechanical ventilation with a lung-protective strategy (low tidal volume, limited plateau pressure) is the correct next step — the same principles apply as in non-TB ARDS. ECMO may be considered for refractory ARDS, but only after a trial of invasive ventilation, so proceeding directly to ECMO referral would be premature. Repeating an NIV trial after documented failure instead delays definitive airway management and risks catastrophic deterioration.
questions.topic: TB ARDS NIV failure invasive ventilation lung-protective

questions.question: A 45-year-old HIV-positive man presents with 10 days of subacute fever, headache and vomiting. GCS is 15 but examination reveals a new left abducens nerve palsy. Lumbar puncture shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein. According to the British Medical Research Council staging system, what is the correct grade of his tuberculous meningitis?
options: A: Grade I — GCS 15 is the defining criterion regardless of focal neurological findings
B: Grade III — any cranial nerve palsy mandates the highest severity grade
C: Grade II — requires GCS between 14 and 10 as the primary criterion
D: Grade II — GCS 15 with a focal neurological deficit
E: Grade I — cranial nerve palsies are common in TBM and do not affect staging
questions.explanation: British Medical Research Council Grade I corresponds to GCS 15 without a focal neurological deficit. Grade II corresponds to GCS 14–10 with or without a focal deficit, or GCS 15 WITH a focal neurological deficit. Grade III corresponds to GCS below 10, with or without a focal deficit. This patient has GCS 15 but a new cranial nerve palsy, which is a focal deficit, correctly placing him in Grade II. Interpretations that disregard the focal deficit criterion entirely, that treat the GCS range alone as sufficient without considering focal signs, or that overstage any focal finding straight to Grade III are all incorrect applications of the staging system.
questions.topic: TBM BRC grading GCS focal deficit abducens Grade I II III

questions.question: A 49-year-old patient with an exudative lymphocytic pleural effusion is strongly suspected to have tuberculous pleurisy. Xpert MTB/RIF performed on pleural fluid returns a negative result. What is the most accurate clinical interpretation?
options: A: A negative result does not exclude TB — Xpert sensitivity falls below 50% in pleural fluid, and further testing is required
B: The result confirms bacterial empyema as the more likely diagnosis
C: Testing three additional pleural fluid samples will substantially improve sensitivity
D: TB pleurisy is effectively excluded given the near-perfect specificity of Xpert
E: Xpert MTB/RIF has equivalent sensitivity in pleural fluid as in sputum samples
questions.explanation: Xpert MTB/RIF sensitivity falls below 50% when pleural or ascitic fluid is analysed, compared with over 98% in smear- and culture-positive pulmonary TB. A negative pleural fluid Xpert result therefore cannot exclude TB pleurisy; pleural biopsy or adenosine deaminase testing (cut-off above 60 IU/L) are needed to pursue the diagnosis further. Testing additional pleural fluid samples does not substantially improve sensitivity. Treating a negative result as excluding the diagnosis confuses the test's high specificity (99–100%, meaning a positive result is reliable) with its sensitivity, which is genuinely low for pleural specimens.
questions.topic: Xpert MTB/RIF pleural fluid sensitivity below 50% ADA biopsy

questions.question: A 31-year-old West African man (HIV-positive, CD4 38/mm³) is admitted to the ICU with 5 weeks of fever, night sweats and weight loss. He develops ARDS, septic shock and laboratory evidence of DIC. Chest X-ray shows a bilateral miliary pattern. Blood cultures are negative at 48 hours. Alongside supportive care, what is the single most important immediate therapeutic intervention?
options: A: Escalate to broad-spectrum antibiotics and antifungal coverage pending cultures
B: Initiate empirical four-drug anti-tuberculosis therapy
C: Administer high-dose intravenous corticosteroids for ARDS
D: Defer treatment until Xpert MTB/RIF on BAL confirms the diagnosis
E: Start antiretroviral therapy immediately to restore immune function
questions.explanation: In a patient with a miliary pattern, ARDS, shock, and CD4 below 100/mm³ from an endemic region, disseminated tuberculosis is the most likely diagnosis. Early empirical anti-tuberculosis therapy is associated with improved survival and must not await microbiological confirmation. Antiretroviral therapy is indicated but should follow TB therapy initiation by about two weeks — starting it immediately and concurrently instead risks severe immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome and is not the primary step. Broad-spectrum antibiotics may be co-administered if bacterial co-infection is also suspected, but they are not the primary intervention here.
questions.topic: miliary TB empirical anti-TB therapy CD4 ART timing septic shock IRIS

questions.question: A 37-year-old man with suspected TBM undergoes lumbar puncture: WBC 220/mm³ (90% lymphocytes), protein 3.8 g/L, glucose 1.4 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L). On standing, the CSF forms a visible web-like protein precipitate. Which finding is MOST characteristic of TBM and most helpful in distinguishing it from viral meningitis?
options: A: Formation of a spider's web clot on standing due to protein precipitation
B: Elevated lymphocyte count with complete absence of neutrophils
C: CSF protein concentration above 1 g/L
D: CSF:serum glucose ratio below 0.33
E: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with predominantly mononuclear cells
questions.explanation: Formation of a 'spider's web clot' when CSF stands at room temperature is a classic, highly characteristic finding of tuberculous meningitis, caused by high-protein precipitation. Lymphocytic pleocytosis and a low CSF glucose are both non-specific findings, also seen in viral and cryptococcal meningitis. A CSF protein elevation above 1 g/L is suggestive but likewise not specific to TBM. The spider's web clot, by contrast, provides strong discriminatory support for TBM and is not a feature of viral meningitis, making it the most characteristic finding among those listed.
questions.topic: TBM CSF spider web clot protein lymphocytes glucose viral meningitis

questions.question: A 41-year-old HIV-negative patient with pulmonary TB is started on rifampicin-containing anti-tuberculosis treatment. He is simultaneously diagnosed with invasive pulmonary aspergillosis. Which statement best describes the clinically relevant drug interaction when selecting antifungal therapy?
options: A: When rifampicin and isoniazid are combined, isoniazid's CYP inhibition offsets rifampicin's induction, keeping voriconazole levels adequate
B: Rifampicin markedly induces CYP450 enzymes, reducing voriconazole to potentially sub-therapeutic concentrations
C: Voriconazole dosing should be doubled to overcome the drug interaction
D: Fluconazole is a safe alternative as it is not metabolised by CYP enzymes and is unaffected by rifampicin
E: Rifampicin inhibits CYP450 enzymes, raising voriconazole levels and increasing neurotoxicity risk
questions.explanation: Rifampicin is a potent CYP450 inducer that markedly reduces plasma levels of all azole antifungals, including voriconazole, fluconazole and itraconazole — often down to sub-therapeutic concentrations. Describing rifampicin as a CYP450 inhibitor reverses the actual mechanism, which is induction. When rifampicin and isoniazid are combined, rifampicin's inductive effect outweighs isoniazid's inhibitory effect, so the net result remains reduced, not adequate, voriconazole levels. Fluconazole is likewise an azole substantially reduced by rifampicin's CYP induction, so it is not a 'safe' alternative unaffected by the interaction.
questions.topic: rifampicin CYP induction voriconazole azoles sub-therapeutic aspergillosis

questions.question: A 53-year-old man from Eastern Europe has confirmed MDR-TB (resistant to isoniazid and rifampicin). After 3 months of standard four-drug first-line therapy, sputum cultures remain positive and clinical condition is deteriorating. A trainee proposes adding moxifloxacin to the current failing regimen as a fifth drug. What is the most appropriate management approach?
options: A: Continue the current regimen for another 4 weeks while awaiting full susceptibility results
B: Add both moxifloxacin and linezolid to the failing regimen
C: Replace the entire failing regimen with 4–5 second-line drugs to which the organism is susceptible
D: Add moxifloxacin to the existing regimen as a fifth drug to increase coverage
E: Switch immediately to fluoroquinolone monotherapy as a bridge pending resistance profiling
questions.explanation: The key principle in managing MDR-TB is that individual drugs must NOT be added one at a time to a failing regimen, since doing so risks rapid acquisition of additional resistance to each newly added drug. Instead, the entire regimen must be replaced with 4–5 drugs to which the pathogen is susceptible, guided by susceptibility testing and with infectious disease specialist input. Switching to fluoroquinolone monotherapy would rapidly select for further resistance rather than solving the problem. Adding even two additional drugs to the existing regimen is still insufficient — the complete failing regimen needs to be abandoned, not incrementally supplemented.
questions.topic: MDR-TB failing regimen replace not add second-line bedaquiline linezolid

questions.question: A 52-year-old HIV-negative woman is admitted with BRC Grade II tuberculous meningitis confirmed by Xpert MTB/RIF on CSF. Anti-tuberculosis therapy is initiated. Which adjunctive agent has the strongest evidence for reducing mortality and long-term neurological sequelae in this patient?
options: A: Corticosteroids are contraindicated in TBM as they impair mycobacterial clearance
B: Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day intravenously for 4 weeks with subsequent taper
C: Steroid therapy benefits only HIV-positive patients; evidence is lacking in HIV-negative individuals
D: Prophylactic aspirin 150 mg/day to reduce thromboembolic stroke risk
E: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy (1 g/day for 3 days)
questions.explanation: Adjunctive dexamethasone at 0.4 mg/kg/day (or prednisolone 60 mg/day) reduces mortality and long-term sequelae in HIV-negative patients with tuberculous meningitis, supported by robust randomised trial evidence. Prophylactic aspirin at 150 mg/day does also reduce stroke risk and mortality in TBM, but the strongest overall evidence for mortality reduction remains with dexamethasone. A claim that steroid benefit is restricted to HIV-positive patients states the opposite of the actual evidence: benefit is most clearly established in HIV-negative patients, while data in HIV-positive patients remain controversial.
questions.topic: dexamethasone TBM HIV-negative mortality evidence aspirin stroke

questions.question: A 66-year-old with advanced cavitary pulmonary tuberculosis develops sudden massive haemoptysis (>400 mL within 30 minutes). Oxygen saturation is 79% on 15 L/min non-rebreather mask. What is the most appropriate FIRST management priority?
options: A: Intravenous tranexamic acid and antitussive (opiate) administration
B: Immediate bronchoscopy with topical diluted epinephrine application
C: Emergency bronchial artery embolisation
D: Urgent CT pulmonary angiography to localise the bleeding source
E: Airway control with endotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation
questions.explanation: In massive haemoptysis with life-threatening hypoxaemia, the first priority is always airway control and adequate gas exchange — typically requiring endotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation. Only once the airway is secured can definitive interventions such as CT angiography, bronchial artery embolisation, or bronchoscopy be safely performed. Tranexamic acid and bronchoscopy are useful adjuncts but are not the first priority in a patient with imminent respiratory compromise. Bronchial artery embolisation is highly effective once a bleeding source is identified on CT, but correct sequencing demands airway management first, before pursuing localisation or definitive haemostatic procedures.
questions.topic: pulmonary haemorrhage airway first intubation embolisation tranexamic acid sequencing

questions.question: A 33-year-old HIV-positive man (CD4 count 72/mm³) is admitted with pulmonary tuberculosis and has never received antiretroviral therapy. Anti-tuberculosis therapy is initiated and tolerated. When should ART optimally be started?
options: A: Within 2 weeks of starting and tolerating anti-tuberculosis therapy
B: After completion of the 2-month intensive phase of anti-tuberculosis therapy
C: After confirmed sputum culture conversion to minimise drug interactions
D: After 8 weeks of anti-tuberculosis therapy to reduce IRIS risk
E: Simultaneously with anti-tuberculosis therapy on day one to minimise HIV progression
questions.explanation: Antiretroviral therapy should ideally be initiated within 2 weeks of commencing and tolerating anti-tuberculosis therapy in HIV-positive patients with pulmonary TB, since this reduces mortality, all-cause morbidity and recurrence. An 8-week delay applies specifically and exclusively to TB meningitis, where early ART is instead associated with excess mortality from CNS immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome — it does not apply to pulmonary TB. Delaying ART until the end of the intensive phase of TB treatment or until culture conversion unnecessarily prolongs immunosuppression, which is especially dangerous at a CD4 count below 100/mm³ such as this patient's.
questions.topic: ART timing pulmonary TB HIV 2 weeks TBM exception 8 weeks IRIS

questions.question: For which of the following patients does the WHO strongly recommend urine lipoarabinomannan (LAM) lateral flow assay as a point-of-care TB screening test?
options: A: Any hospitalised patient from a high-incidence TB country, regardless of HIV status
B: An HIV-positive 38-year-old with CD4 count 150/mm³ detected incidentally on screening
C: An HIV-positive 40-year-old with CD4 count 88/mm³ who is seriously ill and hospitalised
D: An HIV-positive outpatient with CD4 count 340/mm³ presenting with a 6-week cough
E: An HIV-negative 55-year-old from a TB-endemic country admitted with suspected pneumonia
questions.explanation: The WHO strongly recommends the urine LAM assay for HIV-positive adults and children with a CD4 count below 100/mm³, and for those who are seriously ill and hospitalised regardless of clinical TB symptoms. The patient with CD4 88/mm³ who is seriously ill and hospitalised meets both criteria. Patients with CD4 counts of 340/mm³ or 150/mm³ exceed the relevant CD4 threshold and are not described as seriously ill, so they do not meet the recommendation. An HIV-negative patient is not an indication for this test at all. Extending the recommendation to any hospitalised patient regardless of HIV status goes too far, since LAM sensitivity is substantially lower in the absence of HIV-associated immunosuppression.
questions.topic: LAM urine assay WHO CD4 below 100 seriously ill hospitalised HIV

questions.question: A 47-year-old patient with tuberculous meningitis develops communicating hydrocephalus on CT. Conservative management with acetazolamide fails to achieve clinical improvement after 4 days. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: A: Intravenous mannitol infusion to acutely lower intracranial pressure
B: Insertion of a permanent ventriculoperitoneal shunt
C: Increase dexamethasone dose to reduce CSF production
D: Add furosemide 40 mg daily to the acetazolamide
E: Repeated lumbar puncture drainage or placement of an external ventricular drain
questions.explanation: When conservative management of communicating hydrocephalus with acetazolamide fails, repeated lumbar drainage or placement of an external ventricular drain is required — this becomes necessary in approximately one third of TBM cases. An external ventricular drain allows both monitoring and drainage of CSF. Ventriculoperitoneal shunting may be appropriate for chronic or refractory hydrocephalus, but it is not the immediate step when acute conservative therapy fails. Mannitol addresses transtentorial herniation specifically, not communicating hydrocephalus, so it is not the appropriate next step here.
questions.topic: TBM hydrocephalus communicating acetazolamide EVD lumbar drainage one third

questions.question: A 73-year-old with confirmed drug-sensitive tuberculosis develops oliguric acute kidney injury (eGFR 8 mL/min/1.73m²). Which of the following first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs does NOT require dose adjustment in severe renal impairment?
options: A: Pyrazinamide
B: Ethambutol
C: All four first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs require dose reduction in severe renal failure
D: Rifampicin
E: Ethambutol and pyrazinamide
questions.explanation: Rifampicin does NOT require dose adjustment in renal failure, and neither does isoniazid. In contrast, ethambutol and pyrazinamide are both renally cleared and require dose adjustment or extended dosing intervals in severe renal impairment to prevent toxicity: ethambutol accumulation causes retrobulbar neuritis, while pyrazinamide accumulation increases the risk of drug-induced liver injury and arthritis. A claim that all four first-line drugs require dose reduction in severe renal failure is therefore incorrect, since rifampicin and isoniazid are given at standard doses regardless of renal function.
questions.topic: anti-TB drugs renal failure NOT dose adjustment rifampicin isoniazid ethambutol pyrazinamide

questions.question: A patient with confirmed pulmonary tuberculosis requires emergency endotracheal intubation for acute respiratory failure. Which respiratory protection measure is most appropriate for the clinician performing laryngoscopy?
options: A: Standard surgical mask with protective goggles
B: N95 (FFP2) filtering facepiece respirator
C: A powered air-purifying respirator (PAPR) is the only acceptable device during intubation
D: N95 (FFP2) respirator with an added face shield
E: N98 (FFP3) filtering facepiece respirator
questions.explanation: N95 (FFP2) masks are sufficient for routine room entry in TB patients. However, N98 (FFP3) masks must be worn during all aerosol-generating procedures — including intubation, extubation, and handling of respiratory secretions — because of the high concentration of infectious aerosols generated during these procedures. Adding a face shield to an FFP2 respirator provides splash protection but does not upgrade its respiratory filtering capacity to FFP3 level. A powered air-purifying respirator provides equivalent or superior protection to FFP3, but it is not the only acceptable device for this purpose, so describing it as the sole option overstates the requirement.
questions.section: Infection Control and Preventing Spread of Infection
questions.topic: TB infection control N98 FFP3 intubation aerosol-generating N95 FFP2 routine

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT consistent with the recognised differences between children and adults presenting with tuberculous meningitis?
options: A: Extrapulmonary tuberculosis occurs in more than 30% of children, independent of HIV status
B: Children have a lower bacillary load in sputum (paucibacillary) than adults
C: Seizures occur in approximately 50% of children compared with approximately 5% of adults with TBM
D: Agitation and delirium are more common in children than in adults with TBM
E: Children metabolise anti-tuberculosis drugs faster and require higher weight-based doses
questions.explanation: Agitation and delirium are in fact infrequent in children with tuberculous meningitis but comparatively frequent in adults — the reverse of a claim that they are more common in children. The remaining statements correctly describe real differences between children and adults: seizures are approximately ten-fold more common in children (around 50%) than in adults (around 5%); extrapulmonary TB affects more than 30% of children regardless of HIV status; children metabolise anti-tuberculosis drugs faster and require higher weight-based doses; and children have a paucibacillary pattern with a lower sputum bacillary load than adults.
questions.topic: TBM children adults agitation delirium seizures extrapulmonary paucibacillary NOT

questions.question: Regarding risk factors for developing tuberculosis disease from Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Silicosis is a recognised medical condition that increases risk of progression to TB disease.

statements.label: B
statements.text: HIV infection is a major risk factor for progression from latent TB infection to active TB disease.

statements.label: C
statements.text: When exposed to TB-containing droplets, 5–10% of individuals become infected with Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Pregnancy and the post-partum period are recognised risk factors for developing TB disease.

questions.explanation: HIV infection, pregnancy and the post-partum period, and silicosis are all explicitly recognised risk factors for progression to tuberculosis disease. The claim about droplet exposure has the figures and concepts confused: on exposure to infected droplets, roughly 20–25% of individuals become infected, while the 5–10% figure instead refers to the proportion of ALREADY-INFECTED individuals who subsequently go on to develop active TB disease during their lifetime — conflating these two distinct statistics is a critical and common error.
questions.topic: TB risk factors HIV silicosis pregnancy 20-25% exposed 5-10% disease

questions.question: Regarding the Xpert MTB/RIF nucleic acid amplification test in the diagnosis of tuberculosis:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The test simultaneously detects genomic sequences conferring resistance to both rifampicin and isoniazid.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Sensitivity falls below 50% when pleural or ascitic fluid is analysed.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Results from unprocessed samples are available within less than 2 hours.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Sensitivity exceeds 98% in smear- and culture-positive pulmonary tuberculosis.

questions.explanation: Results within under 2 hours from unprocessed samples, sensitivity exceeding 98% in smear- and culture-positive pulmonary TB, and reduced sensitivity below 50% in pleural or ascitic fluid are all genuine diagnostic characteristics of the Xpert MTB/RIF assay. However, the test detects rifampicin resistance only, via rpoB gene mutations — isoniazid resistance detection requires separate low-complexity nucleic acid amplification testing. Assuming that both major first-line drug resistances are detected simultaneously by this single test is a classic near-miss error.
questions.topic: Xpert MTB/RIF sensitivity specificity rifampicin resistance only isoniazid pleural

questions.question: Regarding the adverse effects and pharmacological properties of first-line anti-tuberculosis drugs:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Both rifampicin and isoniazid can be administered at standard doses in patients with severe renal impairment without dose adjustment.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The first clinical sign of drug-induced liver injury from anti-tuberculosis treatment is typically an asymptomatic rise in liver enzymes greater than 3 times the normal value.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Ethambutol is the most common causative agent of drug-induced liver injury in anti-tuberculosis treatment.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy can be prevented by co-administration of pyridoxine.

questions.explanation: Rifampicin and isoniazid require no dose adjustment in renal failure, unlike ethambutol and pyrazinamide, which do. Co-administration of pyridoxine at 10–25 mg per day prevents isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy. An asymptomatic rise in liver enzymes above three times the upper limit of normal is indeed the typical early warning sign of drug-induced liver injury. However, DILI from anti-tuberculosis treatment is primarily caused by rifampicin, isoniazid and/or pyrazinamide — ethambutol can contribute but is not the leading causative agent, so identifying it as the most common cause is incorrect.
questions.topic: anti-TB DILI rifampicin isoniazid pyrazinamide pyridoxine renal adjustment

questions.question: Regarding adjunctive therapies in the management of critical illness from tuberculosis disease:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Rifampicin increases cortisol metabolism and may precipitate adrenal insufficiency.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Steroid therapy has a well-established mortality benefit in patients with pulmonary tuberculosis without CNS or pericardial involvement.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day reduces mortality and long-term sequelae in HIV-negative patients with tuberculous meningitis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Prophylactic aspirin 150 mg/day reduces both stroke risk and mortality in patients with tuberculous meningitis.

questions.explanation: Dexamethasone at 0.4 mg/kg/day reduces mortality and long-term sequelae in HIV-negative patients with tuberculous meningitis; rifampicin's CYP450-inducing effect accelerates cortisol metabolism and can precipitate adrenal insufficiency; and prophylactic aspirin at 150 mg/day reduces both stroke risk and mortality specifically in TBM. However, a well-established mortality benefit from steroid therapy in pulmonary TB without CNS or pericardial involvement is not supported by the evidence — this remains controversial, with the clearly established indications for adjunctive steroids being tuberculous meningitis and pericarditis only.
questions.topic: dexamethasone TBM pericarditis steroids pulmonary not established aspirin rifampicin cortisol

questions.question: Regarding the complications of tuberculous meningitis encountered in intensive care:

statements.label: A
statements.text: The syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) is a recognised cause of sodium disturbance in TBM.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Stroke occurs in up to two-thirds of patients with TBM, predominantly affecting the basal ganglia.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Seizures occur with equal frequency in children and adults with tuberculous meningitis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Hydrocephalus complicating TBM is communicating in approximately 80% of cases.

questions.explanation: Communicating hydrocephalus accounts for approximately 80% of hydrocephalus complicating tuberculous meningitis. Stroke, predominantly affecting the basal ganglia, occurs in up to two-thirds of patients with TBM. The syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion is an explicitly recognised cause of sodium disturbance in TBM. Seizures, however, do NOT occur with equal frequency in children and adults — they are far more common in children (around 50%) than in adults (around 5%), a key paediatric distinction rather than an equivalence.
questions.topic: TBM complications hydrocephalus stroke basal ganglia SIADH seizures children adults

questions.question: Regarding infection control measures for patients with pulmonary tuberculosis in the ICU:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Patients with drug-resistant TB or co-existent immunosuppression may require prolonged isolation due to persistent sputum smear positivity.

statements.label: B
statements.text: N95 (FFP2) masks provide sufficient respiratory protection during endotracheal intubation of a TB patient.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Pragmatic de-isolation may be considered after a 2-week course of effective anti-tuberculosis therapy in drug-sensitive TB.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Isolation in a single room with air under negative pressure, exchanged at least 6 times per hour, is recommended.

questions.explanation: Isolation in a single room with negative-pressure air, exchanged at least 6 times per hour, is recommended for patients with pulmonary tuberculosis. Pragmatic de-isolation may be considered after a 2-week course of effective anti-tuberculosis therapy in standard-risk patients with drug-sensitive TB. Patients with drug-resistant TB or co-existing immunosuppression may remain sputum smear-positive for longer and can require prolonged isolation. However, N95 (FFP2) masks provide sufficient protection only for routine room entry — N98 (FFP3) respirators are mandatory during aerosol-generating procedures such as intubation and extubation, so relying on FFP2 alone during these procedures is inadequate.
questions.section: Infection Control and Preventing Spread of Infection
questions.topic: TB infection control FFP2 FFP3 negative pressure 6 air changes de-isolation MDR

questions.question: Regarding the management of HIV and tuberculosis co-infection in critically ill patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In HIV-positive patients with TB meningitis, ART should be initiated within 2 weeks to minimise the risk of early mortality.

statements.label: B
statements.text: All HIV/TB co-infected patients should receive trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) typically manifests approximately 2 weeks after ART initiation.

statements.label: D
statements.text: In HIV-positive patients with pulmonary TB, ART should ideally be initiated within 2 weeks of starting and tolerating anti-tuberculosis therapy.

questions.explanation: In HIV-positive patients with pulmonary TB, antiretroviral therapy should ideally be started within 2 weeks of initiating and tolerating anti-tuberculosis treatment. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis is recommended for all HIV/TB co-infected patients. Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome typically manifests around 2 weeks after starting ART, which is a characteristic and expected timeframe. However, tuberculous meningitis is the critical exception to the 2-week rule: in TB meningitis, ART should instead be delayed until around 8 weeks after starting anti-tuberculosis drugs, since early initiation is associated with excess mortality from CNS immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome.
questions.topic: ART timing TBM 8 weeks pulmonary 2 weeks TMP-SMX IRIS exception

questions.question: Regarding the clinical features and diagnosis of miliary tuberculosis in critically ill patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Procalcitonin serum levels are typically elevated in tuberculosis disease and can help distinguish miliary TB from bacterial sepsis.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Miliary tuberculosis results from haematogenous dissemination, most commonly seeding the lungs, liver, spleen, meninges and kidneys.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Choroidal tubercles on fundoscopy provide valuable diagnostic clues in patients with suspected miliary tuberculosis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: ARDS, disseminated intravascular coagulation and multi-organ dysfunction are recognised presentations of miliary tuberculosis.

questions.explanation: Miliary tuberculosis results from haematogenous dissemination, most commonly seeding the lungs, liver, spleen, meninges and kidneys. Choroidal tubercles seen on fundoscopy provide a distinctive and valuable diagnostic clue in suspected miliary TB. ARDS, disseminated intravascular coagulation and multi-organ dysfunction are all recognised presentations of miliary tuberculosis. However, procalcitonin is typically NOT elevated in tuberculosis disease itself — a normal or low procalcitonin is more consistent with primary TB, whereas an elevated level would instead point towards bacterial co-infection, the reverse of a claim that PCT helps identify miliary TB directly.
questions.topic: miliary TB organs ARDS DIC choroidal tubercles PCT not elevated bacterial

questions.question: Regarding drug-induced liver injury (DILI) from anti-tuberculosis drugs:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Rifampicin, isoniazid and pyrazinamide are the main causative agents of anti-tuberculosis DILI.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Anti-tuberculosis DILI typically presents with an acute rise in serum creatinine as the earliest laboratory finding.

statements.label: C
statements.text: DILI from anti-tuberculosis drugs occurs at an age-dependent incidence of 3–13%.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Concurrent administration of valproic acid or paracetamol increases the risk of anti-tuberculosis DILI.

questions.explanation: Drug-induced liver injury from anti-tuberculosis drugs occurs at an age-dependent incidence of roughly 3–13%. Rifampicin, isoniazid and pyrazinamide are the main causative agents. Concurrent use of valproic acid or paracetamol increases the risk of anti-tuberculosis DILI, as these are recognised co-hepatotoxins. However, anti-tuberculosis DILI does NOT typically present with an acute rise in serum creatinine as its earliest finding — the earliest laboratory sign is instead a rise in liver enzymes above three times the upper limit of normal; creatinine reflects renal rather than hepatic injury and is not the relevant early marker here.
questions.topic: anti-TB DILI incidence 3-13% rifampicin isoniazid valproic acid liver enzymes

questions.question: Regarding the pharmacokinetics and drug interactions of anti-tuberculosis treatment in critically ill patients:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Rifampicin inhibits CYP450 enzymes, while isoniazid induces them.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Rifampicin can reduce blood levels of moxifloxacin when it is used as a second-line anti-tuberculosis drug.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In critically ill patients with impaired gastrointestinal function, intravenous anti-tuberculosis drugs should be preferred until gut function is restored.

statements.label: D
statements.text: When rifampicin and isoniazid are co-administered, the net CYP effect is predominantly inductive.

questions.explanation: In critically ill patients with impaired gastrointestinal function, intravenous anti-tuberculosis drugs should be preferred until gut function is restored. When rifampicin and isoniazid are co-administered, the net CYP450 effect is predominantly inductive, since rifampicin's induction dominates over isoniazid's inhibition. Rifampicin can also reduce blood levels of moxifloxacin when it is used as part of a second-line, MDR-TB regimen. However, describing rifampicin as a CYP450 inhibitor and isoniazid as an inducer reverses the actual mechanisms: rifampicin INDUCES CYP450 enzymes (reducing co-administered drug levels), while isoniazid INHIBITS them (increasing co-administered drug levels) — this reversal is a classic exam trap worth watching for.
questions.topic: rifampicin CYP inducer isoniazid inhibitor IV critical illness moxifloxacin
tuberculosis disease continuum subclinical infectious incipient mortality risk factors early death
questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiological continuum from Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection to tuberculosis disease, and early mortality in tuberculosis disease:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Individuals with subclinical tuberculosis who have no recognised symptoms may already be infectious to others.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Miliary tuberculosis, an APACHE II score above 20, and a symptom duration exceeding 4 weeks are among the recognised independent risk factors for death in tuberculosis disease requiring intensive care.

statements.label: C
statements.text: Bacilli replication remains completely dormant, with no replication at all, throughout incipient tuberculosis.

statements.label: D
statements.text: The majority of tuberculosis-related deaths occur late in the disease course, more than a year after diagnosis, from gradual multi-organ deterioration.

questions.explanation: Patients with subclinical tuberculosis, even without recognised symptoms, may already be potentially infectious once bacillary replication becomes continuous — infectiousness is not contingent on the patient having noticed symptoms. Incipient tuberculosis is characterised by slow or intermittent bacillary replication, not complete dormancy; true dormancy without any replication instead describes the earlier stage of tuberculosis infection. Miliary tuberculosis, mechanical ventilation, shock, nosocomial pneumonia, multi-organ failure, tuberculosis-destroyed lung, an APACHE II score above 20, and symptom duration beyond 4 weeks are all recognised independent risk factors for death. Contrary to a claim of late, gradual deterioration, many tuberculosis-related deaths in fact occur early — within about 21 days of diagnosis — and the majority result from acute cardiorespiratory failure rather than a slow decline.
